params.text:
Support, wellbeing and learning

Something frightening happening while you were on your own is not a small thing, and you are entitled to support afterwards. Many care workers try to absorb it — finish the round, say nothing, get on with it. That is understandable, and it is not what your employer expects of you. Asking for help after an incident is part of doing the job properly, not a sign that you cannot cope.

  • Debriefs: after a serious incident you should be offered a chance to talk it through with your manager, and sometimes with the wider team. A debrief covers how you are, what happened, and what needs to change. It is not a disciplinary meeting.
  • Occupational health: your employer can refer you for advice on fitness for work, adjustments, or a phased return if you have been off.
  • Employee assistance programmes and counselling: many services offer a confidential helpline or short course of counselling that you can use without going through your manager.
  • Peer and manager support: a colleague who knows the round and the person you support often understands the incident better than anyone. Keep confidentiality, but do not carry it alone.

Reactions are often delayed. You may feel fine for days and then find you are sleeping badly, dreading a particular call, snapping at people, replaying the moment, or avoiding that street entirely. Watch for the same signs in colleagues — withdrawal, uncharacteristic lateness, tearfulness, or brushing it off too quickly — and check in on them.

What should change afterwards

An incident is information. Under regulation 3 of the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, your employer must review the risk assessment when it may no longer be valid or when there has been a significant change — and an incident or near miss is exactly that trigger. HSE guidance for lone workers (INDG73) makes the same point: controls are only as good as the last time they were tested against reality.

  • The risk assessment is revisited: for that person, that property, that time of day, or that route — and updated where it no longer holds.
  • The care plan may change: new information about triggers, behaviour, pets, equipment or who else is in the house should be written in so the next worker is not walking in blind.
  • Visit arrangements may change: double-up visits, a different time of day, daylight-only calls, a named worker, agreed parking, or a changed entry arrangement.
  • Occasionally service is withdrawn or suspended: this is a decision for senior managers with the local authority or commissioner, and it should never rest on one worker's shoulders.

All of this depends on people reporting honestly. A reporting culture is about learning and prevention, not blame. You will not be criticised for raising a near miss, for leaving a home you judged unsafe, or for admitting you did not follow the check-in procedure that day. The only thing that genuinely causes harm is the incident nobody hears about.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the gap to complete these sentences about support after an incident.
params.textField: After a serious incident you should be offered a *debrief*, where you talk through what happened and what needs to change. Support through an employee assistance programme is normally *confidential*, so you can use it without telling your manager what you discussed. Your employer must review the *risk* assessment for that visit and update it where it no longer holds. One control that may be added afterwards is a *double-up* visit, so two workers attend together. Reporting exists so the organisation can learn and prevent harm, not to allocate *blame*.
